truth.papers.A25.title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
truth.papers.A25.paper_scope: Investigating the effect of Al content on aging hardening response of Fe-35Mn-xAl-5Cu low-density steels, achieving ultra-high specific hardness through precipitation of β-Mn phase.

prediction.papers.A25.title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
prediction.papers.A25.paper_scope: Systematic investigation of Al content effects (15–19 at.%) on aging hardening behaviors, microstructural evolution, and property balances in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels through isothermal aging at 525–650 °C.

prediction.alloys.alloy_15al.alloys_notes: Measured composition (at.%): Fe 44.6, Mn 35.2, Al 15.0, Cu 5.2. Density: 6.85 g·cm⁻³.
prediction.alloys.alloy_17al.alloys_notes: Measured composition (at.%): Fe 43.2, Mn 34.8, Al 17.0, Cu 5.0. Density: 6.76 g·cm⁻³.
prediction.alloys.alloy_19al.alloys_notes: Measured composition (at.%): Fe 41.6, Mn 34.8, Al 18.6, Cu 5.0. Density: 6.66 g·cm⁻³.

truth.processes.proc_solution.description: Homogenization at 1200°C for 24 h, hot-rolling to 4 mm thick plates, solution treatment at 1100°C for 10 min under argon atmosphere followed by oil quenching.
truth.processes.proc_aging.description: Following the solution treatment described in proc_solution, aging at temperatures between 525°C and 650°C for 1, 3, 6, or 10 h under argon atmosphere, followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_aging.processes_notes: The same pre-treatment was applied to all alloys before aging.

prediction.processes.proc_aging.description: Vacuum induction melting in argon, homogenization at 1200 °C for 24 h, hot-rolling to 4 mm plates, solution treatment at 1100 °C for 10 min with oil quenching, and aging at 525–650 °C for 1–10 h with water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_17al_01.description: Vacuum induction melting in argon, homogenization at 1200 °C for 24 h, hot-rolling to 4 mm plates, solution treatment at 1100 °C for 10 min with oil quenching, and aging at 525–650 °C for 1–10 h with water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_19al_01.description: Vacuum induction melting in argon, homogenization at 1200 °C for 24 h, hot-rolling to 4 mm plates, solution treatment at 1100 °C for 10 min with oil quenching, and aging at 525–650 °C for 1–10 h with water quenching.

truth.structures.sample_15al_sol.overall_struc: Single α (BCC) phase with equiaxed grains
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_525_3h.over: Predominantly β-Mn (85.8% by area) with untransformed α regions and some α-Mn particles
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_525_3h.micr: fine dispersed particles within α matrix
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_550_6h.over: Complete β-Mn matrix with fine BCC precipitates at β-Mn interfaces
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_550_6h.micr: fine particles distributed at β-Mn interfaces
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_650_3h.over: Completely transformed β-Mn with new FCC phase at interfaces
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_650_3h.micr: fine particles at β-Mn interfaces, contains FCC twins
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.over: Peak-aged sample: β-Mn matrix with FCC Cu-rich phase at interfaces, α matrix completely dissolved
truth.structures.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.micr: fine particles evenly distributed at β-Mn interfaces, containing FCC twins
semi-coherent or incoherent interfaces with β-Mn
truth.structures.sample_17al_aging_525_10h.ove: Only ~20.5% β-Mn precipitated along α grain boundaries and within α grains; some α-Mn particles present
truth.structures.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.over: Almost complete transformation to β-Mn, with new BCC and FCC phases distributed at interfaces
truth.structures.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.micr: fine particles at interfaces, FCC twins observed
truth.structures.sample_19al_aging_525_10h.ove: Only 12.1% β-Mn precipitated, minimal hardening; α matrix with some α-Mn particles
truth.structures.sample_19al_aging_650_6h.over: α matrix almost completely transformed to β-Mn, with a great amount of new BCC phase within β-Mn

prediction.structures.sample_15al_aging_525_3h.over: α matrix with extensive β-Mn precipitation
prediction.structures.sample_15al_aging_525_3h.micr: short rod-shaped along α grain boundaries and interiors
prediction.structures.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.over: Nearly complete β-Mn transformation with fine BCC and FCC Cu-rich precipitates at β-Mn interfaces
prediction.structures.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.micr: fine particles at β-Mn interfaces with FCC twins
prediction.structures.sample_15al_aging_650_3h.over: β-Mn matrix with FCC phase distributed within β-Mn
prediction.structures.sample_17al_aging_525_10h.ove: α matrix with minor β-Mn along grain boundaries and α-Mn particles
prediction.structures.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.over: Nearly complete β-Mn with FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates
prediction.structures.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.micr: fine particles at β-Mn interfaces with twins
prediction.structures.sample_17al_aging_650_10h.ove: β-Mn matrix with minor BCC and FCC precipitates
prediction.structures.sample_19al_aging_525_10h.ove: α matrix with minor β-Mn precipitation
prediction.structures.sample_19al_aging_600_10h.ove: Nearly complete β-Mn with BCC and FCC precipitates
prediction.structures.sample_19al_aging_650_6h.over: β-Mn matrix with abundant BCC phase and FCC Cu-rich precipitates

truth.interfaces.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.phas: During aging, β-Mn precipitates and grows, expelling Al and Cu. Cu-rich FCC phase forms at β-Mn interfaces due to limited Cu solubility. BCC phase also forms at interfaces due to Al enrichment.
truth.interfaces.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.phas: Similar to 15Al alloy: Cu and Al expelled from β-Mn form secondary phases at boundaries. Increased Al content leads to higher amount of secondary phases.

prediction.interfaces.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.phas: α → α + FCC Cu-rich + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC → β-Mn + BCC + FCC Cu-rich
prediction.interfaces.sample_17al_aging_575_6h.phas: α → α + FCC Cu-rich + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC → β-Mn + BCC + FCC Cu-rich
prediction.interfaces.sample_19al_aging_600_10h.pha: α → α + FCC Cu-rich + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC → β-Mn + BCC + FCC Cu-rich

truth.performance.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.com: various low-density steels, conventional steels, medium/high entropy alloys

prediction.performance.sample_15al_aging_600_6h.com: Existing low-density steels, stainless steels, and M/HEAs

truth.characterization_methods.sample_15al_sol: Phase identification and grain orientation mapping
Solution-treated 15Al alloy consists of single α (BCC) phase.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_15al_agi: 85.8% β-Mn formed, with α matrix still present and some α-Mn particles.
α fully transformed to β-Mn; fine BCC phase observed at β-Mn interfaces.
Identify new FCC phase at high temperature aging
No untransformed α; β-Mn and new FCC phase distributed within β-Mn.
Measure intrinsic hardness of β-Mn and overall micro-hardness
β-Mn intrinsic hardness ~12.1 GPa; FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases lower hardness.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_17al_agi: Only 20.5% β-Mn precipitated; α-Mn particles present in α matrix.
Measure intrinsic hardness of β-Mn in 17Al
truth.characterization_methods.sample_19al_agi: Only 12.1% β-Mn; α-Mn particles present; minimal hardness increase.
Phase analysis at high-temperature aging for 19Al
α almost completely transformed to β-Mn; great amount of new BCC phase inside β-Mn.
Measure intrinsic hardness of β-Mn in 19Al
truth.characterization_methods.idx_14.key_find: Both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks detected, indicating co-precipitation before full transformation.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_15al_agi: Nanoindentation (Agilent G200, 200 nm depth, spacing ≥5 μm)
Intrinsic hardness of phases in peak-aged 15Al
Average nano-hardness 11.7 ± 0.4 GPa. β-Mn intrinsic hardness 12.1 ± 0.3 GPa. FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases showed lower hardness than β-Mn.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_15al_sol: Vickers hardness (HVS-1000, 9.8 N, 15 s, 5 repeats averaged)
Bulk hardness measurements across all aging conditions

Solution-treated hardness increased with Al content. Peak hardness values: 965.6 HV (15Al), 924.9 HV (17Al), 917.8 HV (19Al). Full aging hardening curves span 253.4–965.6 HV for 15Al, 259.8–924.9 HV for 17Al, and 263.8–917.8 HV for 19Al.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_17al_agi: Nanoindentation (Agilent G200, 200 nm depth, spacing ≥5 μm)
Intrinsic hardness of phases in peak-aged 17Al
Average nano-hardness 11.1 ± 0.6 GPa. β-Mn intrinsic hardness 12.2 ± 0.1 GPa. Higher error than 15Al due to increased soft precipitates.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_19al_agi: Nanoindentation (Agilent G200, 200 nm depth, spacing ≥5 μm)
Intrinsic hardness of phases in peak-aged 19Al
Average nano-hardness 10.4 ± 1.3 GPa. β-Mn intrinsic hardness 12.5 ± 0.4 GPa. Largest error indicating highest content of soft FCC/BCC precipitates.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Calculation of K_IC from Vickers indentation
Used to evaluate fracture toughness of peak-aged samples from crack lengths. E elastic modulus, HV Vickers hardness, P load (9.8 N), c average crack length.

prediction.computational_details.sample_15al_aging_: Validated against indentation crack length measurements
Calculate K_IC from Vickers indentation crack lengths
E (elastic modulus): 164 GPa for 15Al, 175 GPa for 17Al, 181 GPa for 19Al (from compression stress-strain curves)
c: average crack length (0.25 Σ l_i, where l_i is radical crack length from indentation corners)

K_IC = 0.016 (E / HV)^{1/2} P / c^{2/3}. Average crack lengths from hardness tests: 18 μm (15Al), 22 μm (17Al), 35 μm (19Al). Calculated K_IC: 8.6 MPa·m^{1/2} (15Al), 6.6 MPa·m^{1/2} (17Al), 3.5 MPa·m^{1/2} (19Al).

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fe-35.2Mn-15Al-5.2Cu low-density steel achieved an ultra-high specific hardness of 141 HV·cm³/g.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: The optimal aging temperature range to achieve ultra-high hardness increased with Al content: 525–600°C for 15Al, 550–600°C for 17Al, and 575–625°C for 19Al alloy.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: The increase in Al content slowed down the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn during low-temperature aging, attributed to enrichment of Al around β-Mn and stabilization of D0₃ phase.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The volume fraction of secondary phases (FCC Cu-rich and BCC) at β-Mn interfaces increased with Al content (3.9% for 15Al at 650°C/3h, 4.7% for 17Al at 650°C/10h, 6.5% for 19Al at 650°C/6h), which reduced overall hardness.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The fracture toughness K_IC decreased with increasing Al content (8.6, 6.6, 3.5 MPa·m^1/2 for 15Al, 17Al, 19Al peak-aged samples respectively), partly due to reduction of ductile FCC Cu-rich phase.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: EDS of β-Mn in peak-aged samples showed Al content in β-Mn increased from 14.3 at.% (15Al) to 17.5 at.% (19Al), while Mn content remained around 36.2–36.8 at.% and Cu around 2.6–2.8 at.%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The alloys can be formed in the soft solution-treated state and subsequently aged to achieve desired hardness, potentially simplifying manufacturing of high-hardness wear-resistant components.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The full Vickers hardness ranges from solution-treated to peak-aged states are: 15Al 253.4 HV to 965.6 HV (600 °C/6 h), 17Al 259.8 HV to 924.9 HV (575 °C/6 h), 19Al 263.8 HV to 917.8 HV (600 °C/10 h).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Optimal aging temperature ranges for ultra-high hardness shifted to higher temperatures with increasing Al content: 15Al 525–600 °C, 17Al 550–600 °C, 19Al 575–625 °C.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Nanoindentation average micro-hardness values for peak-aged samples: 11.7 ± 0.4 GPa (15Al), 11.1 ± 0.6 GPa (17Al), 10.4 ± 1.3 GPa (19Al). Error increased with Al content due to higher volume fraction of softer FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates between β-Mn interfaces.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Crack lengths generated during Vickers hardness testing of peak-aged samples: 18 μm (15Al), 22 μm (17Al), 35 μm (19Al).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Specific hardness values from Fig. 16: Fe-35.2Mn-15Al-5.2Cu achieved ~141 HV·cm³/g, surpassing conventional low-density steels (~40), pure iron (~20), and medium/high-entropy alloys (~100).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: SEM-EDS measured Al content in β-Mn for the hardest samples: 14.3 at.% (15Al), 16.3 at.% (17Al), 17.5 at.% (19Al); other element contents were very close among the three alloys.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates in selected high-temperature aged samples: 3.9% for 15Al (650 °C/3 h, hardness 852.3 HV), 4.7% for 17Al (650 °C/10 h, hardness 833.2 HV), 6.5% for 19Al (650 °C/6 h, hardness 810.9 HV).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The D0₃ phase was discussed as potentially present in the α-matrix after solution treatment in high-Al alloys, but was not observed in peak-hardness samples, suggesting its depletion accompanies β-Mn growth during aging.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: XRD samples for phase verification were specifically selected as: 15Al aged at 550 °C for 1 h, 17Al aged at 575 °C for 1 h, and 19Al aged at 550 °C for 6 h, where both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks coexisted.
